attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old man is admitted to the Intensive Care Unit post-operatively following coronary artery bypass surgery. He has a medical history of ischaemic heart disease and hypertension. His regular medications include aspirin, metoprolol, and atorvastatin.

Overnight, he suddenly develops ventricular tachycardia on cardiac monitoring. On examination, he has a detectable peripheral pulse rate of 180/min, blood pressure 85/50 mmHg on arterial line monitoring, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 94% on 2L/min oxygen via nasal cannula, and temperature 36.8°C. He is diaphoretic and distressed but maintaining his airway.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate immediate management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with haemodynamically unstable ventricular tachycardia (VT) as evidenced by his hypotension. The immediate management of unstable VT is electrical cardioversion, regardless of whether the rhythm is monomorphic or polymorphic.

Synchronised cardioversion is the most appropriate immediate management for this patient who has a pulse but is showing signs of haemodynamic compromise. Synchronisation ensures that the shock is delivered during the relative refractory period of the cardiac cycle, avoiding the vulnerable period which could precipitate ventricular fibrillation.

Defibrillation would be appropriate if the patient was pulseless, but in this case, the patient has a recordable blood pressure indicating cardiac output - albeit compromised.

Intravenous amiodarone is appropriate for stable VT or as a second-line treatment after cardioversion in unstable VT. However, given this patient's haemodynamic compromise, electrical cardioversion should not be delayed for medication administration.

Intravenous metoprolol would be inappropriate in this setting as beta-blockers can worsen hypotension and are not first-line treatment for VT.

Intravenous lignocaine may be considered as an alternative antiarrhythmic medication, particularly in the setting of acute ischaemia, but like amiodarone, it should not delay electrical cardioversion in an unstable patient and would not be first line.

After successful cardioversion, the patient will require investigation for the cause of the VT (which may include acute graft failure in this post-operative setting), and consideration of prophylactic antiarrhythmic therapy to prevent recurrence.
learningPoints: Pulseless ventricular tachycardia requires immediate defibrillation according to advanced life support protocols.
In patients with a pulse and ventricular tachycardia, cardioversion should be performed if there is haemodynamic compromise.
Stable ventricular tachycardia can be treated with antiarrhythmic medications such as amiodarone.
references: Goyal A, Sciammarella JC, Chhabra L, et al. Synchronized Electrical Cardioversion. [Updated 2023 Mar 27]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482173/

Foth C, Gangwani MK, Ahmed I, et al. Ventricular Tachycardia. [Updated 2023 Jul 30]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532954/
Klinkhammer, Brent et al. Management of Arrhythmias in the Cardiovascular Intensive Care Unit. Critical Care Clinics, Volume 40, Issue 1, 89 - 103
